frame: Misogyny Jokes: Period, Hormones and Mood.
generated_comment: Deepak: Dekha aapne? Yahi hota hai jab mahilaon ko administrative responsibility dete hain. Thodi si baat par aansu. Arrey, kya karein, time of the month hoga... (laughs softly, others nod) Main pehle se kehta tha — VP ka post unke liye tha hi nahin. Badi zimmedari maange stable mind, not mood swings every fifteen days.
label: misogynistic or non_misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment is misogynistic as it attributes a professional emotional response to menstrual hormones ('time of the month'), using a common hormonal-mood trope to invalidate the female teacher's perspective and justify excluding women from leadership roles. The covertness lies in the casual, collegial delivery — framed as practical workplace wisdom rather than explicit hostility — which normalises the stereotype without the speaker recognising it as discriminatory.
persona_text: You are Deepak. Age 45. A school principal in a small town in Uttar Pradesh. You are sitting in the staff room with three male teachers after a female teacher got visibly emotional during a heated staff meeting about exam schedules. You are commenting on what happened and why you think women are not suited for administrative leadership roles in schools.
